evaluation_inputs.system_description: The CAAS is a common avionics system designed for a family of U.S. Army Special Operations helicopters (A/MH-6, MH-47, and MH-60). It aims to provide a single, open, scalable architecture to modernize avionics, replacing proprietary legacy systems. The system manages cockpit workload, enhances situational awareness ('aviate, navigate, and communicate'), and is built on a foundation of commercial standards to facilitate portability and third-party upgrades.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The CAAS was already well into development at the time of evaluation. Evaluators performed a pre-exercise review of architecture documentation to capture approaches.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How does the cyclic executive ensure real-time performance?
How is the system partitioned to ensure availability and safety?

hardware_view_description: Hardware is portable between platforms ('plug and play' architecture).
Applications are location-transparent and can migrate to other hardware for load balancing.
Every application is resident on every box.
runtime_constraints: Inviolable timing and memory walls between applications (POSIX partitioning).
Applications must not overrun processing time limits or memory allotments.

guaranteed_resources.sockets: Number of sockets is known and guaranteed

questions: Does the partitioning strategy successfully isolate failures?
Do layers effectively isolate high-level graphics services?
Does the client-server approach effectively decouple system parts?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Invalid data is entered by the pilot, and the system does not crash.
Invalid data comes from an actor on any bus, and the system does not crash.
When a 1.9-second power interruption occurs, the system will execute a warm boot and be fully operational in 2 seconds.
A loss of Doppler occurs, the pilot is notified, and the Doppler timer begins a countdown.
A partition fails, the rest of the processor continues working, and the system continues to function.
The data concentrator suffers battle damage, and all flight-critical information is still available.
The mission processor in the outboard MFD fails, and that display and the rest of the system continue to operate normally.

business_goals: Minimize systems acquisition and sustainment costs
Reduce the total ownership cost over the life of the avionics system
Modernize the system with minimal impact to Army Special Operations readiness
Ensure that new CAASs work well with other programs
technical_constraints: Large set of applicable standards (ARINC 661, POSIX, CORBA, IEEE P1386/P1386.1, OpenGL, DO 178B)
Must support multiple helicopter platforms (A/MH-6, MH-47, MH-60)

architectural_approaches.description: Definition of a partition using 'brick-wall partitioning' (POSIX) to establish inviolable timing and memory walls between applications.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Prevents conflicts and crashes from propagating.
Allows applications to be changed independently.

architectural_approaches.description: Applications can share only their state via the network using Remote Service Interface (RSI) and Remote Service Provider (RSP) to isolate network implementation details.
Accessing components only via their interfaces is strictly followed.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Eliminates back-door exploitation of implementation details.

architectural_approaches.description: Used to partition and isolate high-level graphics services.
A flight-ready operating system that's consistent with POSIX and has a standard POSIX API and Ada 95 support.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Provides Level-A design assurance and standard API.

architectural_approaches.description: For flight-critical functions, redundant software is introduced using a master/slave protocol to manage failover.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Ensures critical functions continue during failure.

architectural_approaches.description: Applications do not know where other applications reside; location is bound at configuration time.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Unaffected when applications migrate to other hardware.
Simplifies load balancing and scheduling changes.

children.scenario_description: Invalid data is entered by the pilot, and the system does not crash.
Invalid data comes from an actor on any bus, and the system does not crash.
When a 1.9-second power interruption occurs, the system will execute a warm boot and be fully operational in 2 seconds.

children.refinement: Graceful degradation in the presence of failures
children.scenario_description: A loss of Doppler occurs, the pilot is notified, and the Doppler timer begins a countdown.

children.refinement: Graceful degradation in the presence of failures
children.scenario_description: A partition fails, the rest of the processor continues working, and the system continues to function.

children.refinement: No degradation in the presence of failures for which there are redundant components/paths
children.scenario_description: The data concentrator suffers battle damage, and all flight-critical information is still available.

children.refinement: No degradation in the presence of failures for which there are redundant components/paths
children.scenario_description: The mission processor in the outboard MFD fails, and that display and the rest of the system continue to operate normally.

scenarios.scenario_text: Changes to the CAAS are reflected in the simulation and training system concurrently with the airframe changes, without coding it twice.
No single point of failure in the system will affect the system's safety or performance.
Multiple versions of the system must be fielded at the same time. Those versions should be distinguishable and should not have a negative impact on the rest of the system.
75% of the CAAS is built from reused components increasing new business opportunities.
Given maximum 'knob twiddling' to the level that the system's performance is degraded, the system can prioritize its flight-critical functions, so they are NOT degraded.
Given the need for a second ARC231, the radio can be incorporated into the existing system by reusing existing software at minimal or no cost.
An application doesn't crash, but starts producing bad data. The system can detect the errant data and when applications crash.

scenario_reference.text: Overall evaluation of architectural approaches against quality goals (Composite of 15 scenarios analyzed).

analysis_records.stimulus: Various (Hardware failure, New requirements, System integration)
analysis_records.response_measure: System stability, correctness, and meeting performance budgets.
analysis_records.reasoning: The architecture was found to be well partitioned. POSIX provides strong guarantees for timing and memory, ensuring availability. Locational transparency allows flexible deployment.

risks.description: OFP has no built-in hooks to aid in simulation/training capability.
risks.rationale: This hinders the goal of making the same operational software drive both the simulators and the actual helicopters.

risks.description: Unknown performance requirements and certain performance goals not being met.
risks.rationale: Several analyzed scenarios dealt with performance and revealed risks about unknown requirements.

non_risks.description: The number of sockets used by the system is known and guaranteed.
non_risks.rationale: Allows reliable performance estimation to be carried out.

tradeoff_points.parameter: Letting users set I/O parameters (e.g., turbine gas temperature limits)
